request.question: Which tower was funded through the EB-5 visa program, the Wall Street Tower or the Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower?

title: Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower
Protective Life
Arrivé
Hedden Construction Company
EB-5 visa
Flatiron Building
Wall Street Tower
Lyceum Theatre (Park Avenue South)
Physicians Mutual
Abbas Hashmi
sentences: The Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower, colloquially known as the Met Life Tower, is a landmark skyscraper located on Madison Avenue near the intersection with East 23rd Street, across from Madison Square Park in Manhattan, New York City.
Designed by the architectural firm of Napoleon LeBrun & Sons and built by the Hedden Construction Company, the tower is modeled after the Campanile in Venice, Italy.

The hotel located in the clock tower portion of the building has the address 5 Madison Avenue, while the office building covering the rest of the block, occupied primarily by Credit Suisse, is referred to as 1 Madison Avenue.
Protective Life Corporation is a financial service holding company in Birmingham, Alabama.
The company’s primary subsidiary, Protective Life Insurance Company, was established in 1907 and now markets its products and services in all 50 states.
As of December 31, 2016, the corporation had more than 2,700 employees, annual revenues of $4.48 billion and assets of $75 billion.

In addition to Protective Life Insurance Company, Protective Life Corporation's subsidiaries include West Coast Life Insurance Company, MONY Life Insurance Company, Protective Life And Annuity Insurance Company, ProEquities Inc./Protective Securities, and Lyndon Property Insurance Company.
Arrivé ( ) is a 440 ft , 41-story skyscraper in the Belltown neighborhood of Seattle, Washington, that began construction in April 2015.

The $190 million project, originally named Potala Tower after the Potala Palace in Tibet, was designed by Weber Thompson and consists of 342 apartments and a 142-room hotel operated under the Hotel Indigo brand.
It was financed partially by Chinese nationals through the EB-5 visa program and is scheduled to be completed in January 2019.

Some of the finest buildings in New Jersey, New York City, and other large eastern cities were built by the Hedden Construction Company, one of the largest construction companies operating in Newark in the very early 1900s.
Among the most notable is the Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower located at One Madison Avenue in New York, NY.
The tower was the world's tallest building from 1909 to 1913 and home to the Hedden Construction Company's main offices located on the 36th and 37th floors.
During this prosperous period over $40,000,000 in construction contracts and payments were collected by the firm.

The EB-5 visa, "employment-based fifth preference category" or "EB-5 Immigrant Investor Visa Program", created in 1990 by the Immigration Act of 1990, provides a method for eligible Immigrant Investors to become lawful permanent residents — informally known as "green card" holders — by investing at least $1,000,000 to finance a business in the United States that will employ at least 10 American workers."
Most immigrant investors who use the EB-5 program invest in a targeted employment area (TEA) — a rural area or area with high unemployment — which lowers the investment threshold to $500,000.
The EB-5 program is intended to encourage both "foreign investments and economic growth."
The EB-5 Immigrant Investor Visa Program is one of five employment-based (EB) preference programs in the United States.

The Flatiron Building, originally the Fuller Building, is a triangular 22-story steel-framed landmarked building located at 175 Fifth Avenue in the borough of Manhattan, New York City, and is considered to be a groundbreaking skyscraper.

Upon completion in 1902, it was one of the tallest buildings in the city at 20 floors high and one of only two skyscrapers north of 14th Street – the other being the Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower, one block east.
The building sits on a triangular block formed by Fifth Avenue, Broadway, and East 22nd Street, with 23rd Street grazing the triangle's northern (uptown) peak.
As with numerous other wedge-shaped buildings, the name "Flatiron" derives from its resemblance to a cast-iron clothes iron.
Wall Street Tower is a mixed-use building under development in the Financial District of Manhattan.
The building is being developed by Lightstone Group and was designed by British architect David Adjaye.
The building will be funded through the EB-5 visa program and through more traditional sources of capital.
The Lyceum Theatre was a theatre in New York City located on Fourth Avenue, now Park Avenue South, between 23rd and 24th Streets in Manhattan.
It was built in 1885 and operated until 1902, when it was torn down to make way for the Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower.
It was replaced by a new Lyceum Theatre on 45th Street.
For most of its existence, the theatre was home to Daniel Frohman’s Lyceum Theatre Stock Company, which presented many important plays and actors of the day.
Physicians Mutual is a privately held insurance company headquartered in Omaha, Nebraska, United States, that consists of Physicians Mutual Insurance Company and Physicians Life Insurance Company.
Founded as Physicians Mutual Insurance Company in 1902 by Edwin E. Elliott, Physicians Mutual began by selling health insurance to medical professionals.
Policies were offered to the general public starting in 1962, and by 1970 the company expanded into life insurance when it founded Physicians Life Insurance Company.
Today the company offers a variety of insurance products, annuities, Medicare, Medigap, Medicare Supplement, Term Life Insurance, Whole Life Insurance, Cancer and funeral pre-planning services.
It holds over US$3 billion in assets and employs over one thousand people.
Robert A. Reed is chief executive officer and president.
Abbas Hashmi is an investment banker most well known for founding Green Card Capital.
Hashmi is an industry specialist and supports the EB-5 visa program and the use of transparency and compliance to run the program.
He works with investors, foreign nationals, and high net worth individuals and their families with the EB-5 visa program.
Hashmi has worked for AIG as regional head for the Middle East region.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- If summary_1 already includes explicit profession/role/descriptor tokens for an anchor (for example, 'singer', 'director', 'country music artist'), omit those redundant descriptor tokens and emit a compact anchor-only query consisting of the core visible anchors (names, titles, groups). Retain relation tokens only when the question explicitly requests them or when disambiguation rules demand them. Do not remove descriptors when the question explicitly asks for role/occupation evidence or when a short-title disambiguation rule requires adding an entertainment disambiguator.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity (or otherwise names candidate/bridge entities), treat the context claim as the summary anchor and perform explicit identity-alignment checks before using passage facts. For each entity explicitly named in `context` as a candidate or bridge, do the following: if at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append a single brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that linking fact and references the corroborating passage and also include one short clause for that context-named entity giving the passage-supported relation value (e.g., "Name: [relation value]."); if no passage provides the relation value for that context-named entity, append a short clause stating that no relation value is provided in the evidence (e.g., "Name: no [relation] provided in evidence."). Do not create such clauses for entities that are only mentioned in `passages` and are not named in `context`. Do not append corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep these additions short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.
- Vocal-role single-token normalization (narrow scope): If summary_2 (alone or composed with summary_1 where identity links are explicit) explicitly contains vocal-role evidence — i.e., one or more verbalized vocal-role phrases that clearly denote a singing performer (examples: "lead singer", "lead vocals", "vocalist" as presented in the summaries) — then you may prefer to emit the concise single-token common noun for a person who sings (e.g., "singer") only when that single-token common noun itself appears verbatim in the summaries or when the vocal-role phrases in the summaries unambiguously denote the same person-role. If those explicit lexical licenses are not present, retain the literal role phrasing as given in summary_2 and do not normalize modifiers or drop qualifiers.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Which tower was funded through the EB-5 visa program, the Wall Street Tower or the Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower?
hop1_docs: Wall Street Tower | Wall Street Tower is a mixed-use building under development in the Financial District of Manhattan.  The building is being developed by Lightstone Group and was designed by British architect David Adjaye.  The building will be funded through the EB-5 visa program and through more traditional sources of capital.

Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower | The Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower, colloquially known as the Met Life Tower, is a landmark skyscraper located on Madison Avenue near the intersection with East 23rd Street, across from Madison Square Park in Manhattan, New York City.  Designed by the architectural firm of Napoleon LeBrun & Sons and built by the Hedden Construction Company, the tower is modeled after the Campanile in Venice, Italy.  The hotel located in the clock tower portion of the building has the address 5 Madison Avenue, while the office building covering the rest of the block, occupied primarily by Credit Suisse, is referred to as 1 Madison Avenue.

100 Montgomery Street | 100 Montgomery Street also known as the Equitable Life Building is an office tower located in the financial district of San Francisco, California.  The 354 ft , 25-floor tower was completed in 1955 and served as headquarters to the Equitable Life Insurance Company.

AXA Towers | The AXA Towers are two commercial office buildings in Syracuse, New York.  AXA Tower I was built in 1966 and Tower II was built in 1971, and both stand at 81.7 meters and have 19 floors.  They are tied for the second tallest buildings in Syracuse.  They were both built with a tube-in-tube structural system with a steel and glass curtain wall facade.  The buildings serve as offices for New-York-based AXA Equitable Life Insurance Company, and have illuminated logos on top.

Morden Tower | The Morden Tower in Back Stowell Street on the West Walls of Newcastle upon Tyne, England, is a Scheduled Ancient Monument and a Grade 1 listed building.  Since June 1964, Connie Pickard has been custodian of Morden Tower, and has made it a key fixture of Newcastle's alternative cultural life, with the building hosting music and poetry events often funded by Pickard.

Double Tower | The Double Tower is one of the 17 towers which were part of the city walls of Waterford, Munster, Ireland.  It was built in the latter part of the 15th century and the early part of the 16th century.  Six of the original 17 towers survive to this day; the other five being Reginald's Tower, Beach Tower, Semi-Lunar Tower, French Tower, and Watch Tower (Waterford).  The Double Tower is located on Castle Street, between the French Tower and the Watch Tower.  All three towers are well-preserved, along with some parts of the old city wall between them.
Tower Insurance | TOWER Insurance is a New Zealand-based insurance company which provides fire & general insurance.
response.summary_1: Passage [1] states the Wall Street Tower "will be funded through the EB-5 visa program." Passage [2] (Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower) contains no mention of EB-5 or funding sources — next hop: retrieve sources or funding history for the Met Life Tower to confirm whether it was ever funded via EB-5.
response.hop2_query: Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower EB-5 visa funding
hop2_docs: Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower | The Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower, colloquially known as the Met Life Tower, is a landmark skyscraper located on Madison Avenue near the intersection with East 23rd Street, across from Madison Square Park in Manhattan, New York City.  Designed by the architectural firm of Napoleon LeBrun & Sons and built by the Hedden Construction Company, the tower is modeled after the Campanile in Venice, Italy.  The hotel located in the clock tower portion of the building has the address 5 Madison Avenue, while the office building covering the rest of the block, occupied primarily by Credit Suisse, is referred to as 1 Madison Avenue.

Wall Street Tower | Wall Street Tower is a mixed-use building under development in the Financial District of Manhattan.  The building is being developed by Lightstone Group and was designed by British architect David Adjaye.  The building will be funded through the EB-5 visa program and through more traditional sources of capital.
Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Hall of Records | Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Hall of Records

Segregated fund | A Segregated Fund or Seg Fund is a type of investment fund administered by Canadian insurance companies in the form of individual, variable life insurance contracts offering certain guarantees to the policyholder such as reimbursement of capital upon death.  As required by law, these funds are fully segregated from the company's general investment funds, hence the eponym.  A segregated fund is synonymous with the U.S. insurance industry "separate account" and related insurance and annuity products.

Pacific Life | Pacific Life Insurance Company is an American insurance company providing life insurance products, annuities, and mutual funds, and offers a variety of investment products and services to individuals, businesses, and pension plans.  Pacific Life also counts more than half of the 100 largest U.S. companies as clients.

EB-5 visa | The EB-5 visa, "employment-based fifth preference category" or "EB-5 Immigrant Investor Visa Program", created in 1990 by the Immigration Act of 1990, provides a method for eligible Immigrant Investors to become lawful permanent residents — informally known as "green card" holders — by investing at least $1,000,000 to finance a business in the United States that will employ at least 10 American workers."  Most immigrant investors who use the EB-5 program invest in a targeted employment area (TEA) — a rural area or area with high unemployment — which lowers the investment threshold to $500,000.  The EB-5 program is intended to encourage both "foreign investments and economic growth."  The EB-5 Immigrant Investor Visa Program is one of five employment-based (EB) preference programs in the United States.

BT Financial Group | BT Financial Group is a financial services company based at Tower Two, International Towers Sydney in Sydney, Australia, with operations there and in New Zealand.  Its product range includes managed investments, life insurance, superannuation and discount brokering products.  BT also offers custody and settlement services to institutional customers and fund managers.
hop2_titles: Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Hall of Records
response.summary_2: Passage [2] explicitly states the Wall Street Tower "will be funded through the EB-5 visa program" (Wall Street Tower: funded via EB-5). The Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower has no EB-5 funding mentioned in the provided evidence (Metropolitan Life Insurance Company Tower: no EB-5 funding provided in evidence).
